speaker: interviewer
text: --1995, And we're in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came back from Warsaw. He didn't find anything, of course. It was still everything in ruins. And he asked me what I want to do, because he's going to Lodz to this family to live with them, because he was all alone. And the whole family was dispersed during the-- the younger people were in the army, resistance army. We didn't know what happened to them. We did not know what the other parts of the family, where they went.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was-- he decided to go back to Lodz to this cousin. And he told me, look. If you want, because for him and for the other, I was from Lwow. Yes? And Lwow by then was elected by the Russian. So he told me, if you want to go to Lwow and try to go home, I can take you as far as Krakow. And then, you can go east. So we went to Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it did not occur to me to go to the city and to have a look if some Jews are there. By chance, my husband was there. Was attack. And I did not believe that there are some Jews still in Poland. I thought that I am the only one to survive. If I sold myself at this stage as a Jew. Because I wiped out from my mind everything that could endanger me. And I spoke like they did, and the mentality started to change. If you live with someone, especially in a crisis like that, you really try to assimilate their way of life. Anyway, we got to the station to catch a train which goes to Lwow. There are no trains. There are only coal trains. And there stands a group-- you will tell about luck. There stand a group of people, young people. A couple with a small child the same age than Michael and two brothers of hers. Three men-- Polish men. Strong, big. They had one look at me, and they adopted me. And they took me in, and they put me on the coal train with their sister and the baby. And they were coming from Germany from forced labor. All of them. And they had suitcases full of food like that. So it finished that when they were feeding their baby, my baby had to eat the same thing. When they were feeding their sister, I had to eat the same thing. When we had to change the train, and we were changing those coal trains I don't know how many times, eight days and eight night we were traveling in this winter from the Krakow to Przemysl the 200 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Open coal train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Open train.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far is that in distance?

speaker: interviewee
text: 200 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight days and eight nights. And the minute that we had to change the train, they took off their sister with the baby, next me with the baby, next the food. And so we went rolling like that towards this. They come from Jaroslaw. That is a small town not far from Przemysl, 30 kilometers from Przemysl. And when we arrived there, they had-- they went out. But I had to promise them solemnly that if I got to Lwow or I will not be able to get through the ration to Lwow, I have to come back to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they think you were a Catholic still? Polish Catholic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was still Polish Catholic. So I was lucky with people. Really lucky. So I went towards Przemysl, and the train stopped five kilometers before in the country, and I had to walk with the baby those five kilometers. And he was 20 months old at this time. And I was, I don't know if I was 32 kilos. You see, I was carrying him.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I arrived. I arrived to the house where we lived before the war. 3 [NON-ENGLISH]. I gave you that. And I thought, now I'm going to ask the janitor what happened, if somebody is alive, if somebody is this. And I up and knock at the door of the janitor, and a stranger opens the door. And I asked her, where is [NON-ENGLISH]? That was her name. And angrily, she told me, the whole family was killed by the Germans. And she slammed the door in front of me.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was at the end of my rope. I just sat there on the steps in the entrance to the house, and the first time in such a long time, I started to cry. I just didn't know. I was so tired. I was so cold. I was so exhausted emotionally. And I just was sitting there and crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then suddenly, I lifted my eyes and I saw a plaque on the opposite house, on the opposite side of the street, proclaiming Dr. Rosenfeld, dentist. Now Rosenfeld is a Jewish name. And I remembered that there was a dentist before the war in Przemysl called Rosenfeld. So I ran there, and she opens the door. And she recognized me at once, and she asked me, does Stazy know that you are here? Like I would be yesterday, you know, somewhere close. And if he knows that I am here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not know that he's alive, because we did not-- we were not in touch one and a half year, you know. Anyway, they started to clean up the child. He was all black from those coal. He gave him the first glass of milk after such a long time. And their daughter ran-- she knew where my husband is and she ran to tell him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had they stayed in your town the whole time? The dentist?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know where they where. I never asked. You see, because I was not long in Przemysl anymore. And so he came. And, of course, the joy of reunion, and then he told me that he saw my sister when he went-- she went through south of Poland with the Czech army and this. And then so he had a small room with a friend of his. He took over my sister's flat in the house where we lived. But he did not need a big flat like that. So he took one room. In the two rooms was the kitchen, which were like ours, small rooms, was the woman with her daughter who survived with him in hiding. And in the other room there were some four kids, Polish kids. Their parents were shot because they were hiding Jews. And so the Jewish community took care of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. First of all, I had to eat. First of all, I had to get a bit normal. And after a certain time, I had one look around, and I told my husband, I am not going to stay in Poland. I know them better than you. I was one of them, and they did not hide from me. What is our future in Poland? So we-- in meantime, somehow-- I don't know how. There were no frontiers yet, no borders. People were going from one country to another, the soldiers and-- somehow my sister got the news that I am back, and she sent me a message through a soldier, because she was quite a big shot in the army. She's sent me-- This is your Rushka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rushka who lives now in Los Angeles. She sent me a message, come right as you are to me. So to make a long story shorter, because my husband got sick in meantime, so we had to stay. In it was April, we left for Prague. I had only--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is April '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, '45. I had-- I was still crossing the border with my German papers, although I had the certificate from the Jewish community that I'm Jewish. And we crossed to-- we went to Prague. My husband was short time with us, but he had to go back to Poland because he had some unfinished business. Because they, in south of Poland, they were liberated much, much earlier than I was. And see, I was still nearly to the end, you know. It was on the other side. But they were liberated much earlier. But to illustrate that how the Poles never thought-- one thought that I could be Jewish. I was standing-- I did not know the value of money then. I did not know how to buy a-- I did not have money for such a long time. But anyway, one day I went to the marketplace to buy something to eat. And there stands a peasant woman with a chicken. And next to her stands a Jewish girl-- girl-- she was a woman my age, you know. And she recognized me, and I recognized her all right. She was bargaining for this chicken with this peasant woman. And I approached. I wanted to know what is the price of it. I didn't know absolutely what the meaning of money. So the peasant woman got with her upset, took the chicken out of her hands, and gives it to me. And she tell, I prefer to give to you the chicken for 500 zloty than to this Jewish person for 1,000. And she gave it to me for 500 zloty. You see, after the war, they still-- nobody thought that I am Jewish. And especially now, when I had this Warsaw accent, you know, that was a typical Polish-- they saw that is somehow, I don't know, I did not have those special look of a Polish person. But I did not have the look of a Jew, especially not the behavior, which was very, very important. Anyway we started to-- this April, in April we went. My husband come back to this, and I stayed with my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague. Nine months. Then my husband come back from Poland, and we try to get away, because the communists started to take over Czechoslovakia. So we knew that it is time for us to move on. And meantime, my brother in France somehow found us. I think through Red Cross or something. And he got in touch with us to tell us to come to France. We did not have much. We certainly did not speak French. But I told my husband my foot will not stand on German soil. I will not go to the DP camp. I was not in camp the whole time, and I will not be in Germany. Like I did not want to be in Poland. There are two countries which I am not going to stay in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went-- we had wanted to go to France, and I don't have a passport. I don't have any papers. How I come to be in Prague? So I went to the Polish consulate. Then I have to explain. So they tell me where you want to go? Go back to Poland. Show me a visa where you want to go. So I went to the French consulate. The French consulate told me, how can we give you the visa if you haven't got a passport? So I was running from one place to another. I had no passport. I had no visa. Then the Polish consul told me, how come-- how you come here with a baby? So I wrote him a poem, a book, how I come to be-- suddenly I come to Prague with a baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there my husband, when he came, he found a connection with the Paraguayan consul. And he gave us visas, and we got the Polish passport. We went to France. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go in France?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Paris. My brother was in Paris. He took care of us, and that was what-- which was not so good for us, because he did not want to do anything with the Jewish organizations. Because our people we knew, they were sitting in good hotels paid by Joint and [NON-ENGLISH]. And he did not want-- he told us he doesn't want anybody from those organization this. So we didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in France, we had to learn the language. We had to learn to earn the living. We did not have anything. I did not have any profession, any trade. I did not know anything apart from a bit of knitting. My husband was a lawyer. What a lawyer can do in a foreign country, in a foreign language and that? So he had to learn something practical. I had to learn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael was three years old. So I took him in the morning to a child minding center, and I run to Alliance Francaise which was situated not far from Sorbonne. That was for the foreign student on the level of university for the-- I went around-- I run over there. At 12 o'clock, I was running back picking up the child and cooking the meal, because the French are eating twice a day. They are eating cooked meals. So I had to cook the meal. And we lived by then-- I lived on the outskirts of Paris. My brother bought a house there. At 2 o'clock, I had to be in the center, not far from the opera, because I enrolled into a school of design and dressmaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who supported you in the meantime?

speaker: interviewee
text: In meantime my brother helped us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband was working?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was learning, too, because he could not work. What do you-- he was learning this handbags making art. The Jewish organization-- art. So at 2 o'clock, I was [NON-ENGLISH]. And at 5 o'clock-- till 5 o'clock until I took the underground now back, I had to pick up the baby at 6 o'clock and start to cook again. And it was not an easy time, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you work there as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. I worked. Because when I finished the course, I was so good at it that the head of the school, this woman. I hardly spoke yet French. I spoke a little bit what I learned at this, but that was that at the beginning. And I did not have, of course, a permit to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she offered me a position with Vogue patterns. I come from Przemysl, from a very backward country. I didn't know what is Vogue, I didn't know what was patterns, I did not know what it is. So I told her no. I told her I haven't got work-- she told me everything will be taken care of. And that was my first big mistake which I made, because I didn't know what it is and I told her no. Because I wanted to learn, try it, really saw that I can support myself. You see? So I finished the school. I found a place with a Polish dressmaker, a good dressmaker. And I learned there practical thing, and I then when she left, all her customers stayed with me. And I was working privately. The high class was one of a kind, you know, for those Jewish woman from Poland who had those ideas that they have to have the best. And they had enough money then.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was working like that. Meantime my husband established himself. The same thing, you see. And he started to earn well. So I stopped to work. I got very sick in the meantime. But I stopped work. I kept only two or three good and pleasant customers, with whom I went very friendly. Just not to forget what I-- and I had nothing to do the whole day. By now, Michael was in school, you know, and we bought a flat in Paris. And we loved every minute of it. Money or not money, hunger or not hunger at the beginning, but we really loved it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then in '52, before '52 when it was the Korean War, and the panic settled all over Europe. Everybody who could get out, from our people, just run to Canada, to America, wherever they could. So my husband told me we have to go as well, because Europe can't defend itself. If Russia is moving, there is nobody to stop them. So we have to go out, to get out. So we got out. How did you get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who organized your papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: UNRRA. Not the Jewish organization. UNRRA.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the international something relief organization for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they organized your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --passage to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Passage to Australia. And we took the last ship which they organized. It means that we did not pay anything. On contrary, they gave us $100 pocket money with us on top of that. And in February 1952, we came to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- it was a very small ship. Some of the ferries here are bigger than that ship was. That was a ship designed by the Chinese for the rivers, but the French rented it somehow, and they were transporting their-- because by then, they had all those colonies in the Pacific and this. So they were transporting their officials and other people there. And so they put 300 of us, we were there, refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trip was more than two months. A beautiful trip. The nicest in my life. And the best-- you see, we left all our troubles behind. We did not know what's awaiting us. Our minds were absolutely free. We were on a friendship like at home, because we all spoke French freely and very well. And we stopped at every French colony. And not French, even. We stopped at Madeira and Guadalupe and Martinique and this and Suez Canal and what's time of this that ship this before Suez Canal. And everywhere. Then we stopped in Tahiti for more than a week, 10 days. And then New Caledonia, New Hebrides, everywhere we had a lovely time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In February-- 19 February 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you land?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how was your life in Sydney when you landed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the beginning, it was beautiful. We had still some money. We had rented somewhere, because friends who sent us the papers they had to show that we had somewhere to stay. So they rented the room somewhere for us. And the weather was glorious, beautiful. The beaches was beautiful We did not yet this-- and when we were on the ship, we were told that here there is such a lack of workers, that people are standing on the airport and in the port. They are taking people from the ships to work. My luck ran out. The minute we landed here, there is a depression. They were throwing out people from work everywhere. But somehow, it took us a long time, you see, till we got used to the new way of life, the new mentality. Language, for example. The new habits. Absolutely when we were eating, they hold those forks differently. We forgot that. They were laughing when my son went to school with a briefcase. You see, but they were laughing when we were walking on the left side instead of the-- it was very difficult. And, of course, there was not a Jewish community. There was not a welfare state. Nobody to help you. Mostly that we had a so-called sponsor. Our sponsors were only one year here, and they did not know much themselves. But anyway, we had to really absorb all those difficulties to come over. And you know, somehow it took us longer than we expected. It took us quite long. But at the end, we finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had the factory. So my husband started first, and he said this. But somehow the handbag were not good here, and we found someone that was this-- he went, like everybody else at this time, was going to petty market. And he met there a man. And they started to talk. And he told the man, you know, my wife is a dressmaker. So he told him, really. I need some blouses. If she can make me some blouses, you can't find any decent blouse here in Sydney.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was-- we went to a shop. We didn't know where to go. We bought a piece of material in the retail of course. And I was standing on this in the kitchen-- on the kitchen table I cut with the scissors the dozen blouses he wanted. I sew it. I brought with me a small home machine, you know-- and I made those blouses. I embroidered those buttonholes by hand. He got those dozen blouses. He sold in no time. Then two days later, he comes. Perhaps she can make me two dozen blouses? I made him two dozen blouses. So then when he came back, he wanted more. So I told my husband, why he has to sell my blouses? You take the blouse and go and sell it. And he went. He went to Paddington. He went to one shop. The first shop he went in. The woman there was a Jewish shop. The people were from here from before the war. They were big wholesalers, which he didn't know because that was a small shop. When she saw the blouse, he rings me back after half an hour and he tells me, you know I got an order for 1,000 blouses. From one place. I nearly fainted. I said, where I am going to buy the material? Where I'm going to buy the lights? I need it for this. Who is going to make those 1,000 blouses? I'm not going-- where we are going to cut it? So somehow we resolved all those problems, and we were on our way. I had very good neighbors. We found a flat at the beginning. We were very hard up this-- because in those days, you could not rent a place with a small child. Small. Michael was not so small anymore. He was 9 and 1/2 years old. But they heard the child, that was it. No decent place would let it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did not-- we had enough money to put a deposit on a house. But when I saw those little red brick houses with the little gardens in front of them, so I told I never lived in the country. I will not know how to live in a house like that. I want a flat. So we got-- finally we got a flat in Clovelly. And I will tell you, that was a blessing. We were--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape eight of the interview with Pepa Rosenman. It's the 18th of December, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia.
